title: 怪钟疑案
summary:
Dick arrives at a location, claiming he is there because his apartment requires renovation and his doctor suggested the seaside air. He reveals he specifically requested room 413. He informs the narrator, Colin, that he has called the county police commissioner and is waiting for Hercule Poirot.

When Poirot arrives, the group sits together. Dick attempts to discuss the investigation, but Poirot interrupts, claiming he is also there because his London apartment needs renovation. Poirot suggests that Colin brought him the case not to solve it, but to challenge and mock him by providing a problem that cannot be solved simply by sitting in an armchair.

Poirot claims to know who killed the man at 19 Wilbraham Crescent and who killed Ingrid Bret, though he admits he has not yet found the evidence to prove it. He explains that "being certain" means that once the correct method is found, the facts will fall into place. Poirot compares the need for a detective to the need for a hunting dog that can track and please its owner. He describes a "dog" with extraordinary abilities—a metaphor for a source of information—that possesses an extraordinary memory and can repeat specific, accurate details of conversations and events as if it were present at the scene.

Poirot and Colin discuss the inconsistencies of the case, noting that newspaper reports are often inaccurate. They review the facts of the death at 19 Wilbraham Crescent: a middle-aged man was found dead, carrying a business card for an R.H. Curry of a non-existent insurance company. The investigation notes that a stenographer, Miss Hillar Webber, was requested by a Miss Mylees Pemmerton to go to the residence at three o'clock, where she discovered the body.
